title: Outdoor Planter Steps or Benches
summary: A two-tier 2x8 platform built to work as garden steps or a stacked bench — 45°-mitered 2x4 top trim and 2x6 treads dress up a genuinely simple stacked-box structure.
description:
This is two simple 2x8 boxes stacked on each other, sized so the top step sits back far enough from the bottom step's edge to actually be walkable — or, since both boxes are just as happy holding soil as bearing weight, the same structure doubles as a two-tier garden bed or a stacked bench depending on where it lands in the yard.

The two steps use slightly different lumber on purpose: the bottom step is built from 2x8s while the top step also uses 2x8s, which makes the bottom step 1-1/2" taller than the top — a detail the source is upfront about accepting if the base will be surrounded by grass anyway. If matched step heights matter for your use, swap 2x6s into the bottom step instead, since a plain height mismatch stops being invisible once there's no grass to hide the difference.

Build both boxes with self-tapping deck screws, then set the top step on the bottom and adjust for square before adding the treads and trim. The 45°-mitered 2x4 top trim is the one part that needs real corner-cutting care — everything else is straightforward butt joints in construction lumber. **Use materials genuinely rated for outdoor and ground-contact exposure** — anything less breaks down fast sitting directly on soil or grass.

cutList.note: Swap for 2x6 stock if you want the bottom step to match the top step's height.
Both ends cut at 45°, long point to long point measurement.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body:
Everything here is a square butt joint except the top trim, so cut the boxes first and save the miters for last. From the 2x8s: two bottom-step long sides and one top-step long side at 96" — that is the full length of an 8 ft board, so use your straightest stock and do not count on squaring a damaged end. Then nine ends at 18" (five for the bottom box, four for the top), two top-step sides at 21" and the top-step back at 54". From the 2x4s: three internal supports at 7-1/2" and eight tie-in trim pieces at 14-1/2", which the cut list marks 'cut to fit' — leave those long and trim them at assembly. From the 2x6s, four treads at 54", two per 10 ft board.

Leave the eight top trim pieces until the boxes are together. They are 22-1/2" long point to long point with both ends at 45°, so set the miter saw once and cut all eight without touching the setting, then dry-fit four of them as a closed frame before cutting the rest.

steps.title: Build the bottom step
steps.body:
Stand the two 96" 2x8 long sides on edge and parallel, and set the five 18" ends between them — two at the ends and three spaced along the run as internal dividers, because a 96" span of 2x8 will bow under soil or a footstep with nothing behind it. Drive two 2-3/4" exterior deck screws through the long side into the end grain of each end piece.

The finished box is 96" long by 21" deep once the two 1-1/2" sides are counted in. Measure both diagonals and rack it until they match before the last screws go tight. If you want the two step heights to match exactly, substitute 2x6 stock for this box's long sides and ends — the box drops 1-1/2" and nothing else changes.

steps.title: Build the top step
steps.body:
Start the top box with its 96" 2x8 long side and the four 18" ends, set out the same way as the bottom box, and add the three 7-1/2" 2x4 internal supports where the box needs backing behind a joint. Then close it up with the two 21" sides and the 54" back.

The 54" back is what sets the width of the walking surface — the four 54" treads land on it later, so that dimension has to come out right. Two 2-3/4" screws per joint, and check the diagonals again before anything is final; this box has to sit flat on the one below it, and a racked box will only touch on three corners.

steps.title: Stack and square
steps.body:
Set the top box on the bottom box and slide it back far enough that the bottom step is left as a walkable tread in front — that setback is the whole point of the two-tier layout, and it is builder's choice rather than a dimension the plan gives. Both boxes are the same 96" length, so line their ends up flush and adjust front-to-back only.

Measure corner to corner across the top box to confirm it is square to the one underneath, then hold it there while you fasten. Stand back and sight down the whole 96" run first: a top box set slightly out of parallel is invisible now and very obvious once the trim goes on.

steps.title: Attach the trim and treads
steps.body:
Fit the eight 14-1/2" 2x4 tie-in pieces first, trimming each to the gap you actually have — the cut list marks them 'cut to fit' for a reason, since they bridge two boxes whose exact relationship you set in the last step.

Then set the mitered top trim: dry-assemble each closed frame of four 22-1/2" pieces, check every 45° corner closes with no gap, and only then fasten with 2-3/4" screws. Finish with the four 54" 2x6 treads across the top, spaced evenly so water runs through instead of pooling. Use ground-contact-rated stock and exterior screws everywhere — this sits directly on soil or grass full time, and anything less breaks down fast.

images.alt: Two-tier outdoor planter steps built from 2x8 lumber
